skill.name: convex-performance-audit
skill.description: Audits and optimizes Convex application performance across hot-path reads, write contention, subscription cost, and function limits. Use this skill when a Convex feature is slow or expensive, npx convex insights shows high bytes or documents read, OCC conflict errors or mutation retries appear, subscriptions or UI updates are costly, functions hit execution or transaction limits, or the user mentions performance, latency, read amplification, or invalidation problems in a Convex app.
skill.summary: Diagnose and fix performance problems in Convex applications with structured audits and targeted optimizations.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: All 264 static findings are false positives triggered by Convex-specific terminology. The scanner misidentified TypeScript code examples in markdown as shell commands, Convex database operations as SQLite access, and database table names as cryptographic algorithms. No malicious behavior detected. The skill is a legitimate performance optimization guide for Convex applications.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at multiple locations (agents/openai.yaml:3, references/function-budget.md:15,19,67, references/hot-path-rules.md:107,240, references/subscription-cost.md:16,244, SKILL.md:3,28,97). These are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanner detected Convex-specific terminology: 'digest tables', 'table indexes', 'table scans', 'hash patterns' in database design contexts. Convex is a TypeScript backend framework using its own database abstraction, not cryptographic systems. No actual weak cryptography is present.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct analysis of flagged lines confirms they contain legitimate Convex terminology like 'digest table', 'index', 'table scan' in database optimization contexts. No cryptographic algorithms are present.

high_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution Detection
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 196 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' across all reference files. These are FALSE POSITIVES. All detections occur in TypeScript/Convex code blocks within markdown documentation (SKILL.md, references/*.md). Backticks are used for code formatting in markdown. Examples: `ctx.db.query()`, `.filter()`, `useQuery()`. These are legitimate code examples showing Convex API usage, not shell commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct inspection of flagged lines shows markdown code blocks containing TypeScript/Convex code. Backticks are markdown syntax for code formatting, not Ruby shell execution operators.

high_findings.title: SQLite Database File Detection
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'SQLite database file' at multiple locations. These are FALSE POSITIVES. References to `ctx.db.query()`, `ctx.db.get()`, `ctx.db.patch()` are Convex's own database API, not SQLite. Convex is a TypeScript backend framework with its own document database abstraction. No SQLite files are accessed.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Convex uses a proprietary TypeScript database API accessed via ctx.db. The scanner incorrectly matched 'db' in code patterns to SQLite terminology.

high_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'System reconnaissance' at a few locations. These are FALSE POSITIVES. Detections occurred in table scanning patterns like `.collect()`, `.query('items')`. These are legitimate Convex database operations for querying document tables, not system scanning or enumeration.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns are standard database table queries in Convex. No system enumeration or reconnaissance behavior is present.

high_findings.title: Heuristic Dangerous Combination Flag
high_findings.description: Static scanner raised '[HEURISTIC] DANGEROUS COMBINATION: Code execution + Network + Credential access'. This is a FALSE POSITIVE. Network activity consists of legitimate documentation URLs (docs.convex.dev). No credential exfiltration or unauthorized network access exists. Code examples in markdown are Convex API demonstrations, not malicious execution patterns.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The 'network' evidence is a single documentation URL. No exfiltration or credential theft patterns exist. The skill provides legitimate performance optimization guidance.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variable Reference
medium_findings.description: references/subscription-cost.md:73 contains `import.meta.env.VITE_CONVEX_URL`. This is legitimate environment variable access in a Vite/Convex application. VITE_ prefixed variables are public by design in Vite. No secrets are accessed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: VITE_ prefixed variables are client-side and intentionally public. This is standard Vite/Convex configuration, not credential access.

content.user_title: Optimize Convex app performance with structured audits
content.value_statement: Convex applications can develop performance bottlenecks as they scale. This skill diagnoses performance issues across hot-path reads, subscriptions, OCC conflicts, and function limits, then applies targeted fixes following established patterns.
actual_capabilities: Diagnose read amplification issues by tracing database reads and identifying unnecessary scans or joins
Optimize reactive subscriptions to reduce unnecessary invalidation and re-renders
Resolve OCC conflict errors and write contention through document splitting and transaction optimization
Fix function budget violations by batching mutations, limiting return values, and moving heavy work to actions
Apply migration-safe index rollouts that handle partial backfills without correctness bugs
Audit sibling functions for consistent performance patterns across table families
limitations: Cannot modify the Convex schema directly without human review of migration implications
Cannot run `npx convex insights` commands or access deployment health data without user-provided context
Cannot guarantee performance improvements without measured baseline signals or access to insights data
Cannot handle complex migration rollouts without reviewing the full data model and dependencies

use_cases.title: Fix slow dashboard loads
use_cases.description: A developer notices their Convex dashboard takes 3+ seconds to load. The skill traces read paths, identifies unnecessary full-document fetches on hot paths, and recommends digest table patterns to reduce payload size.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers with slow Convex-powered dashboards

use_cases.title: Reduce subscription churn
use_cases.description: A product team sees excessive re-renders and UI lag. The skill identifies pages with too many individual subscriptions, suggests batching queries and using point-in-time reads for low-freshness flows.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers experiencing React performance issues with Convex

use_cases.title: Clear OCC conflict errors
use_cases.description: A developer sees frequent 'conflict' errors in their Convex mutations. The skill audits write patterns, identifies hot documents causing contention, and recommends document splitting or OCC resolution strategies.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers debugging write contention in Convex

prompt_templates.title: Basic performance check
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Convex application for performance issues. Start with the strongest signal available.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have no specific symptoms but want a general audit

prompt_templates.title: Specific slow feature
prompt_templates.prompt: My [page/feature name] in Convex feels slow. The insights show high bytes read. Trace the read path and suggest the smallest high-impact fix first.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have specific performance metrics from npx convex insights

prompt_templates.title: Subscription cost analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze subscription patterns in my Convex app. Focus on reducing unnecessary invalidations and re-renders. Start by identifying the top subscribers and their invalidation frequency.
prompt_templates.scenario: When UI feels sluggish and React profiling shows frequent re-renders

prompt_templates.title: Write contention audit
prompt_templates.prompt: My Convex mutations are hitting OCC conflicts. Audit the write patterns for the [table name] table and suggest how to reduce contention.
prompt_templates.scenario: When seeing conflict errors or excessive mutation retries

output_examples.input: My dashboard page takes 5 seconds to load and npx convex insights shows 2MB of bytes read
output_examples.output:
Diagnosis: Full-document fetches on the projects list query. Each document includes large content fields not needed for the list view.

Recommended fix order:
1. Create a projectDigests table with only list-view fields (title, status, authorName, updatedAt)
2. Update write path to populate digest on each project mutation
3. Switch the list query to read from projectDigests instead of projects
4. Audit sibling list queries for the same pattern

Expected improvement: 90% reduction in bytes read for the list page

output_examples.input: Users report laggy UI even though individual queries are fast
output_examples.output:
Diagnosis: 23 individual useQuery calls on the project detail page, each creating separate subscriptions. Frequent field updates cause cascading invalidations.

Recommended fix order:
1. Batch related data into a single query returning project + author + comments
2. Use 'skip' argument pattern to avoid undefined-arg subscriptions
3. Isolate frequently-updated fields (lastSeen, unreadCount) into separate documents
4. Consider point-in-time reads for low-freshness sections

Expected improvement: 80% reduction in subscription count, fewer re-renders

output_examples.input: Getting OCC conflict errors on my messages mutation
output_examples.output:
Diagnosis: messages table has a global counter document that every message sender increments. High contention on a single document.

Recommended fix order:
1. Remove the counter document pattern entirely
2. Use a reactive count from the @convex-dev/aggregate component instead
3. For leaderboard or ranking use cases, consider periodic recomputation vs live counts

Expected improvement: Elimination of OCC conflicts on the messages table

best_practices: Start with the strongest signal: run npx convex insights --details to identify the actual bottleneck before auditing code
Follow the recommended fix order in each reference file; earlier fixes typically have higher impact with lower risk
Always audit sibling functions touching the same tables to ensure consistent patterns and avoid leaving one path optimized while another regresses
anti_patterns: Applying digest table patterns to small tables or low-traffic paths; the migration and maintenance overhead rarely pays off
Replacing all subscriptions with point-in-time reads; live reactivity is valuable for collaborative or real-time features
Removing indexes without understanding compound index dependencies; a compound index by_foo_and_bar may be needed even when by_foo seems sufficient

faq.question: How does this skill decide which problem to fix first?
faq.answer: The skill starts by gathering the strongest available signal, typically from npx convex insights. It then routes to the most relevant reference file based on the symptom class: hot-path reads, subscription cost, OCC conflicts, or function limits.

faq.question: Will these changes break my existing Convex application?
faq.answer: The skill follows a verification checklist to confirm behavior is preserved. For risky changes like index rollouts, it recommends migration-safe patterns and escalation before editing.

faq.question: What if my performance issue spans multiple problem classes?
faq.answer: Multiple problem classes can overlap. The skill recommends reading the most relevant reference first, then checking others if symptoms remain. It also audits sibling functions across all touched tables.

faq.question: How do I know if a digest table is worth the complexity?
faq.answer: Digest tables are recommended when the path is clearly hot, source rows are much larger than the UI needs, or many readers repeatedly pay the same join cost. For small tables or modest traffic, the overhead is rarely justified.

faq.question: Can this skill run the convex insights commands for me?
faq.answer: The skill cannot execute CLI commands directly. It relies on you to run npx convex insights --details and share the output, or to provide deployment health signals from the Convex dashboard.

faq.question: What happens if my fix requires a schema migration?
faq.answer: If the fix requires schema changes with partial backfill risks, the skill stops and presents escalation options. It references the convex-migration-helper skill for rollout workflows that handle old and new states safely.
